system_context.business_goals: Provide a fully automated test environment for Prolog programs.

architectures.name: PROTest II - Interactive Prolog Test Environment
architectures.description: A modular, pipeline-based architecture for automating the testing lifecycle of Prolog programs. The system processes Prolog source code through a sequential series of specialized components to execute tests, analyze coverage, generate inputs, and produce reports.
architectures.architecture_characteristics: Modularity, Automation, Testability, Deterministic Processing, Specialization

key_components.responsibilities: Analyzes Prolog source code to understand its syntactic and logical structure (e.g., predicates, clauses). This provides the foundational model for subsequent test generation and analysis.
Generates test data (input values and queries) based on the code structure from the Structure Checker and guided by coverage goals.
Executes the generated test inputs against the target Prolog program, capturing the results (success/failure, outputs).
Instruments the source code and analyzes execution traces from the Test Driver to determine which clauses were covered during testing.
Aggregates data from the Test Driver and Coverage Analyzer to produce human-readable reports on test results, coverage metrics, and possibly areas requiring more testing.

architectures.data_flow: The data flows sequentially through a defined pipeline: 1) Prolog source code enters the Structure Checker (C-01). 2) The structural model is passed to the Test Input Generator (C-02). 3) Generated tests are sent to the Test Driver (C-03) for execution. 4) Execution results are shared with both the Test Coverage Analyzer (C-04) and the Test Report Generator (C-05). 5) The Coverage Analyzer also uses the original structural model (from C-01) for instrumentation and analysis, feeding its results to the Report Generator (C-05). This is a primarily linear, forward-flowing pipeline with some data fan-out from the Test Driver.
architectural_constraints: Pipeline execution order is fixed and linear.
Tightly coupled to the Prolog language and its execution model.
Assumes source code is available for structural analysis and instrumentation.
Processing is batch-oriented; not designed for real-time or interactive test execution.
architectures.notes: The architecture is highly specialized for Prolog's logic programming paradigm. Changes to the testing workflow (e.g., order of operations) or support for a new programming language would require significant re-engineering of multiple components, especially the Structure Checker and Coverage Analyzer. Adding new test metrics or report formats would be easier, primarily involving the Report Generator. The architecture CAN easily accommodate: new test input generation heuristics (within C-02), new report formats (C-05), and integration with external test case repositories. The architecture CANNOT easily accommodate: non-Prolog languages, non-sequential/non-batch testing workflows, real-time feedback during test execution, or distributed test execution without major refactoring.

scenario_elicitation.elicitation_method: AI-Driven Brainstorming based on System Context

scenarios.scenario_text: Given a Prolog source file, the system automatically executes all defined unit tests, measures code coverage, and generates a report without any manual intervention, completing within 30 seconds for a 500-line program.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Direct match to current pipeline architecture; all components are designed to work sequentially for automation. Assumes components are integrated to run without breaks.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Stakeholders require full automation to reduce manual effort and increase reliability in testing Prolog programs.

scenarios.scenario_text: A user can interactively create a new test case by specifying input parameters and expected outputs through a GUI, and the system automatically integrates it into the test suite, updating coverage analysis in real-time.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect match; current architecture supports test execution and analysis, but lacks explicit interactive development capabilities. Would require new UI component and tighter feedback loops between components.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Stakeholders seek an interactive environment to enhance test development efficiency and user engagement.

scenarios.scenario_text: The system administrator can configure batch processing for multiple Prolog files overnight, with automated logging and error handling, ensuring 99% uptime during scheduled runs.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect match; pipeline supports sequential processing, but lacks built-in scheduling, logging, and error management features. Would require new orchestration component.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Stakeholders need scalable, reliable operations for large-scale testing without manual oversight.

scenarios.scenario_text: A maintainer can extend the system to support a new Prolog dialect by modifying only the Structure Checker and Test Input Generator components without disrupting the overall pipeline.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Direct match to modular architecture; components are designed for separation of concerns, allowing isolated updates. Assumes well-defined interfaces.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Stakeholders value adaptability to evolving Prolog standards, ensuring long-term usability with minimal rework.

scenario_classification.rationale: The scenario directly aligns with the core purpose and sequential pipeline design of the architecture. All involved components (C-01 through C-05) are designed to work together in a linear, automated batch process to execute tests, measure coverage, and generate a report without manual steps. The 30-second performance target is an implementation/optimization concern, not an architectural mismatch.

The architecture is designed for batch-oriented, linear processing with no interactive feedback loops. Supporting real-time GUI interaction, on-the-fly test suite integration, and immediate coverage updates requires breaking the fixed pipeline constraint and introducing new state management and event-driven communication between components.

While the pipeline can process files sequentially, the architecture lacks built-in capabilities for job scheduling, orchestration of multiple file batches, centralized logging, robust error handling, and system monitoring required for reliable, scheduled overnight runs with high uptime guarantees.

The architecture's modularity and separation of concerns, as noted in the architecture description, allow for isolated updates to language-specific components. The Structure Checker (C-01) and Test Input Generator (C-02) have well-defined interfaces and responsibilities, enabling changes to support a new dialect without requiring modifications to the downstream pipeline components (C-03, C-04, C-05), assuming the interface contracts are maintained.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: No architectural changes are required. The scenario is a direct execution of the existing pipeline. Potential changes would be limited to non-architectural optimizations (e.g., performance tuning, better integration scripting) to meet the 30-second target.
issues_identified: The 30-second performance target for a 500-line program is an implementation/performance requirement that may need optimization within components, but does not necessitate architectural change.
Assumes all components are correctly integrated and can run end-to-end without manual intervention, which is an integration task rather than an architectural one.
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: N/A - The architecture natively supports this scenario.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: 1. Introduce a new 'Interactive GUI/Controller' component to manage user interactions. 2. Modify the Test Input Generator (C-02) to accept manually defined test cases from the GUI in addition to its automated generation. 3. Modify the Test Driver (C-03) and Coverage Analyzer (C-04) to support incremental, real-time execution and analysis triggered by the GUI, breaking the linear batch flow. 4. Modify the Report Generator (C-05) to support partial/incremental report updates. 5. Establish new event-driven or callback-based communication channels between the GUI, C-02, C-03, C-04, and C-05 to enable real-time feedback.
issues_identified: Fundamentally changes the processing model from batch to interactive/event-driven, violating the 'Pipeline execution order is fixed' and 'Processing is batch-oriented' constraints.
Introduces state management complexity (e.g., what is the 'current' test suite state?) that the architecture does not currently handle.
Risk of creating tight coupling between the new GUI and core pipeline components, reducing modularity.
Real-time coverage analysis may require significant changes to the Coverage Analyzer's (C-04) instrumentation and analysis model.
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: 1. Maintain the batch architecture but add a GUI that allows users to define test cases. The GUI would save these to a file, and the standard pipeline would process this file in its next scheduled run. This provides user-friendly test creation but defers integration and analysis, avoiding real-time requirements. 2. Create a 'simulation mode' in the GUI that uses a lightweight, isolated instance of the Test Driver and a simplified coverage estimator to give immediate feedback, while full integration and official reporting still occur via the standard batch pipeline.

affected_components: All components (C-01 to C-05) are affected by the need for external orchestration.
scenario_evaluations.change_description: 1. Introduce a new 'Orchestrator/Scheduler' component external to the core pipeline. This component would be responsible for: job scheduling, managing a queue of Prolog files, invoking the pipeline for each file or batch, handling logging aggregation, implementing error handling strategies (e.g., retries, notifications), and monitoring system resources to ensure uptime. 2. The core pipeline components (C-01 to C-05) would need to be made more robust and idempotent, with improved error signaling and logging to interface effectively with the Orchestrator. Their external interfaces may need standardization for automated invocation.
issues_identified: The architecture itself does not prevent this scenario; the changes are additive (a new wrapper component). However, ensuring 99% uptime is a systemic requirement that depends on the underlying platform and the robustness of all components, not just architecture.
The 'Orchestrator' becomes a critical single point of failure for the automated process.
Error handling and logging are currently implied responsibilities within components; they would need to be formalized and exposed to the Orchestrator.
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Use an existing, battle-tested external scheduler (e.g., cron, Jenkins, Apache Airflow) to orchestrate the pipeline. This approach minimizes changes to the PROTest II architecture itself. The required changes would then be limited to: 1) Packaging the pipeline as a callable service/script with clear exit codes and log output. 2) Possibly creating a lightweight wrapper script to handle file iteration and error logging at the OS/scheduler level.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: No architectural changes are required. As per the architecture notes, changes to support a new Prolog dialect are localized to the Structure Checker (C-01) and Test Input Generator (C-02), provided their interfaces to downstream components remain stable. This is a direct application of the architecture's modular design principle.
issues_identified: Assumes the new dialect's execution model is compatible with the existing Test Driver (C-03) and Coverage Analyzer (C-04). If the dialect introduces new control flow or semantic features, those downstream components might also need updates, increasing the change extent.
The effort estimate (Medium) reflects the complexity of understanding and implementing support for a new dialect, not the architectural impact, which is low.
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: N/A - The architecture's modularity is designed specifically to accommodate this type of change.

scenario_interaction_weights.rationale: C-02 is impacted by three scenarios, including S-02 (interactive GUI) and S-04 (new Prolog dialect), both requiring significant modifications. Failure to address S-02's interactive requirements could severely limit usability for end-users, while S-04 changes could affect maintainability if not properly modularized. The high scenario count combined with S-02's high-risk, high-effort changes makes this the top hotspot.

C-03 is critical for S-02's shift from batch to interactive processing, requiring major changes to support real-time execution. This impacts end-users seeking immediate feedback and developers implementing event-driven flows. Not addressing this could block interactive capabilities entirely, violating core user requirements and increasing technical debt due to architectural mismatch.

C-04 must adapt to S-02's real-time coverage analysis, which involves complex changes to instrumentation and state management. End-users depend on accurate, incremental coverage feedback; delays or inaccuracies could undermine trust in the tool. Developers face high implementation risk due to potential performance and modularity issues.

C-05 requires updates for S-02's incremental reporting, but changes are less foundational than those in C-03/C-04. End-users benefit from real-time report updates, but batch reporting remains viable as a fallback. Developer effort is moderate, focusing on interface extensions rather than core logic changes.

C-01 is affected by S-04's new dialect support, which is a localized, planned change with low risk. S-01 requires only performance tuning. This hotspot is important for maintainability and extensibility but poses less immediate business risk compared to interactive-scenario components.

architectural_strengths: Strength 1: The architecture demonstrates strong modularity and separation of concerns, enabling localized changes for specific extensions. Evidence: S-04 (support for a new Prolog dialect) is classified as Direct and requires only localized modifications to C-01 and C-02, assuming stable interfaces. This indicates the architecture successfully encapsulates dialect-specific logic.

Strength 2: The batch-oriented, linear pipeline architecture robustly supports core automated testing scenarios. Evidence: S-01 (execution of the standard pipeline) is classified as Direct, requiring no architectural changes. The fixed execution order and component independence provide a stable, predictable foundation for the primary business goal of a fully automated test environment.
architectural_weaknesses: Weakness 1: The architecture is fundamentally rigid regarding its processing model, struggling to support interactive, event-driven requirements. Evidence: S-02 (Interactive GUI) is classified as Indirect with Widespread changes, High effort, and High risk. The evaluation notes it violates core 'batch-oriented' and 'fixed pipeline order' constraints, requiring a fundamental shift to an event-driven model and introducing state management complexity.

Weakness 2: The architecture lacks built-in mechanisms for external orchestration and systemic robustness, pushing these concerns outside its boundary. Evidence: S-03 (Continuous Integration) requires the introduction of a new external 'Orchestrator' component. While the change is additive, it highlights that the architecture does not natively address scheduling, error handling strategies, or high-availability concerns, creating a potential single point of failure.
overall_results.modifiability_assessment: The architecture exhibits high modifiability for changes that align with its core batch-pipeline paradigm (e.g., extending supported languages) but low modifiability for changes that require a different processing model (e.g., interactive workflows), indicating a rigid coupling to its foundational execution pattern.

recommendations.recommendation: Adopt the 'simulation mode' alternative approach for S-02 (Interactive GUI) to avoid a high-risk, high-effort re-architecture. Develop a separate, lightweight GUI component that uses isolated, simplified versions of C-02, C-03, and C-04 for immediate user feedback, while the official, authoritative run remains the standard batch pipeline.
recommendations.rationale: This preserves the integrity and simplicity of the core batch architecture while meeting immediate user needs for interactivity. It improves modifiability by containing the complex event-driven logic to a separate, disposable component, preventing tight coupling and state management complexity from polluting the main pipeline.

recommendations.recommendation: Formalize and standardize the error handling and logging interfaces of all core pipeline components (C-01 to C-05) to prepare for external orchestration (S-03). Define clear exit codes, error message formats, and log output locations.
recommendations.rationale: This directly improves modifiability by making the system more composable and easier to integrate with external schedulers (e.g., Jenkins, Airflow). It decouples the pipeline's operational logic from its core functionality, allowing the S-03 requirement to be met with minimal changes to the architecture itself.

recommendations.recommendation: Refactor the Test Input Generator (C-02) to explicitly separate its configuration/interface logic from its core test generation logic. This is informed by its status as the top-priority hotspot, impacted by S-02, S-04, and S-01.
recommendations.rationale: Improves modifiability by making C-02 more adaptable to multiple input sources (automated generation, GUI-defined cases) and new Prolog dialects. A cleaner internal separation of concerns reduces the risk and effort for future changes to this critical component.

recommendations.recommendation: Investigate and document the assumptions the Test Driver (C-03) and Coverage Analyzer (C-04) make about Prolog execution semantics to mitigate risk for S-04 (new dialect support).
recommendations.rationale: Proactively improves modifiability for future language extensions by clarifying hidden dependencies. This low-effort activity reduces the risk that supporting a new dialect will inadvertently require widespread changes beyond C-01 and C-02.
